findings.title: Sensitive Credentials Stored in ConfigMap
findings.description: Task 9 explicitly instructs storing S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID, S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and database connection strings in a Kubernetes ConfigMap. ConfigMaps are not designed to hold sensitive information, are not encrypted at rest by default, and are often exposed to wider RBAC permissions than Secrets.
findings.recommendation: Store all credentials and sensitive connection strings in Kubernetes Secrets. Applications should consume these via environment variables or mounted secret volumes.

findings.title: Kubernetes API Server Exposed via Public Tunnel
findings.description: Task 5 configures a Cloudflare Tunnel to expose the Kubernetes API server to a public hostname. Exposing the cluster's control plane to the internet significantly increases the attack surface and risks cluster compromise.
findings.recommendation: Remove public ingress to the Kubernetes API server. If remote access is required, use a VPN, a secure bastion host, or enforce strict Cloudflare Access (Zero Trust) policies with MFA.

findings.title: Missing Pod Security Contexts for Databases
findings.description: The deployment tasks for PostgreSQL and Valkey do not specify security contexts. Running database containers without dropping unnecessary Linux capabilities or enforcing non-root execution increases the impact of potential container breakouts.
findings.recommendation: Define strict Pod Security Contexts in the Custom Resources for CloudNative-PG and Redis operators. Ensure containers run as non-root users and drop all unnecessary capabilities.

findings.title: Unpinned Operator Dependencies
findings.description: Tasks deploying the CloudNative-PG and Redis operators do not specify version pinning for the Helm charts or YAML manifests. This can lead to deploying unverified or breaking versions in the future.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly pin the versions of the Helm charts or container images used for all operators to ensure reproducible and secure deployments.

summary: The infrastructure plan establishes a solid foundation with planned network isolation and observability, but contains critical security flaws in its initial deployment phases. Specifically, storing infrastructure credentials in plain-text ConfigMaps and exposing the Kubernetes API server to the public internet via a tunnel present immediate, severe risks that must be remediated before execution.
strengths: Network policies are proactively planned for namespace isolation (Task 16).
TLS certificates and Ingress controllers are included in the scaling plan for secure external communication (Tasks 13, 14).
The Valkey deployment uses a pinned, minimal Alpine base image, reducing the container attack surface (Task 7).
Comprehensive observability and alerting are integrated into the production hardening phase (Tasks 17-19).
